title: Learning by pyramiding: how sequential search Increases the problem-solution-fit in open innovation
author: Mehner, Barbara
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Pyramiding is a sequential search technique used to identify individuals with rare characteristics, such as lead users or holders of very specific expert knowledge. Pyramiding has been found to be a quite efficient method for this purpose, as typically only few steps (i.e. referrals) are necessary to arrive at the top of the pyramid (i.e. the individuals searched) (von Hippel et al., 2009; Stockstrom et al., 2016). It also has proven to succeed in accessing analogous markets, i.e. unusual information holders (Poetz & Pruegl, 2010). What is still missing is an investigation of its third property, namely the learning effects enabled by its sequential character.

It also has proven to succeed in accessing analogous markets, i.e. unusual information holders (Poetz & Pruegl, 2010). What is still missing is an investigation of its third property, namely the learning effects enabled by its sequential character.
